story:
“can you keep a secret?” my girlfriend’s rushed whisper yanks me out of a fog. i study her: auburn hair yanked too tight, cracked lips, bloodshot eyes that won’t land on me for long. she’s wringing her hands like she’s hiding something in her own skin. everything about her says trouble. still, i promise to keep quiet, zipping my lips with a shaky smile.

she laces her smaller fingers through mine and pulls me away from school, clinging like i’m a lifeline. i tell myself she’s scared, that whatever she’s done is something i can still help fix if i keep her calm. we walk farther than feels normal, into the woods where the air grows damp and my gut grows loud. i try to read her, but she’s a page stuck shut. the questions in my head are a storm i can’t outrun: what did silvia do? will i have to betray her to protect myself?

she stops deep among the trees beside a messy mound of leaves and dirt. a faint, coppery smell hangs in the air, raw and wrong. she kicks the leaves aside, peels back a blue tarp, and the hole below opens like a mouth. i look down and my legs nearly fold. lorelei stevens—my ex—stares back with glassy eyes that see nothing. her skin is waxy and pale, her lips tinged blue, a jagged wound along her throat still dark with dried blood. my stomach gives in; i gag and heave, spit and bile burning my throat. a hand touches my back and i flinch away, choking on air and shock, on the way silvia’s stare cuts through me.

“she still wanted you, elijah,” silvia says, voice trembling and sure at the same time. “she was a danger to our future. i had to get rid of her. i could never let another girl have you. we’re meant to be.” her icy eyes don’t blink. she looks at me like she’s waiting for me to understand, to choose her, to make this ours. they demand agreement. they promise consequences.

my body decides before my brain does. i turn and run. branches claw at my arms. i hear her behind me—silvia, our school’s best runner—footsteps getting louder, breaths steady. “come on, babe, you said you’d keep your lips sealed,” she calls, playful like this is a game. i make the mistake of looking back. she’s right there. i surge toward the thinning trees, the distant light. hope flares—and then something hard cracks against the back of my skull, a dull, sickening bloom of heat that drops me into black.

when i wake, the world is heavy and close. pain throbs behind my eyes. dirt presses on my chest; more rains down in clumps. i can’t sit up. my limbs feel leaden, my head too thick to lift. i can’t breathe right. i try to scream, but there’s cloth stuffed between my teeth, soaking with spit and fear. above me, silvia shovels, her face set, her hands steady now. each scoop lands like a countdown. i stare up at her and understand: the secret she made me promise to keep is not just lorelei in the ground—it’s me, too.

i thrash until my arms burn. the earth settles in around me, warm and suffocating. silvia’s silhouette shrinks to a shadow rimmed with daylight. i hear the shovel scrape, the dirt hush, my own pulse hammering in my ears. if i had seen the signs sooner—if i had told someone—maybe this ending would be different. i gave my word without knowing what she meant to make of it. i keep it the only way i can, as the soil closes, as her secret—and my breath—are buried together.
